text: Highlight Clip Peter Schiff: Bitcoin May Not Go to Zero, but It Will Underperform Gold On June 16, gold bull Peter Schiff and crypto investor Anthony Pompliano debated on Fox Business. Asked whether he would bet Bitcoin will still exist in 10 years, Schiff refused to bet it would go to zero, but said he would bet it underperforms gold. Pompliano argued that both Bitcoin and gold supporters believe governments will keep printing money, but Bitcoin's higher volatility gives it greater upside. Schiff countered that Bitcoin has seen drawdowns of more than 70%, volatility is not an advantage, and current buying from Bitcoin companies and ETFs is mostly helping early investors cash out. — link

text: JPMorgan: Bitcoin Has Traded Below Production Cost for Five Consecutive Months, Leaving About 20% of Miners Unprofitable Bitcoin mining economics have worsened in 2026, according to JPMorgan. Bitcoin has traded below its estimated production cost of approximately $78,000 for five consecutive months, leaving about 20% of miners unprofitable. Publicly listed mining companies sold more than 32,000 BTC in the first quarter of 2026 to fund operations, exceeding their total bitcoin sales for all of 2025. JPMorgan expects hashrate and mining difficulty to remain highly volatile, with larger and more frequent fluctuations, as long as bitcoin continues to trade below its production cost. — link

text: The Ascent: Own the Substrate — A Map to 2045 The Civilizational Shift to Automated Production • Human society is moving away from labor and energy-constrained economies toward an era of abundant intelligence, automated physical labor, and frontier expansion • This shift structurally decouples economic output from human labor, meaning future wealth will belong to those who own the underlying infrastructure and raw substrates rather than those who sell labor or hold paper currency The Resource Bottlenecks and the National Security Stack • The rapid scaling of these technologies will collide with massive physical constraints, including grid capacity, electricity delivery, and critical mineral supply chains • Because these bottlenecks are essential to national dominance, states will absorb and protect these industries as vital national security assets, creating a hybrid environment where governments de-risk the downside while commercial markets price the output

text: Unlocking The New Industrial Revolution While AI intelligence is advancing rapidly, its full potential to improve living standards remains "trapped" by a lack of physical infrastructure Need to address major shortages in power and energy (straining data centers and the grid) and develop physical action-takers (like robotics and autonomous machinery) that can execute tasks in the real world rather than just digital environments Instead of competing directly with tech giants on building frontier AI models, the most strategic opportunity for founders lies in rebuilding the industrial stack Massive venture capital and hyperscaler demand are shifting toward startups innovating in grid efficiency, hardware-software hybrid energy storage, specialized vertical robotics, and next-generation raw materials like green steel and superconductors

text: New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh's First FOMC Meeting and Press Conference According to The New York Times, newly appointed Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin M. Warsh concluded his first policy press conference on Wednesday following his initial FOMC meeting. Warsh exhibited a sharp stylistic departure from his predecessor Jerome H. Powell's "plain-English" doctrine, leaning heavily into a more political demeanour layered with corporate boardroom jargon, frequently referencing "first principles", "alternative frameworks", and the Fed's "remit." Notably, Warsh confirmed he was the single Fed policymaker who withheld a projection from the "dot plot" metrics, while vowing that the agency remains singularly focused on delivering "price stability." Despite pre-appointment criticisms that Warsh would act as a political proxy to lower interest rates on demand for President Trump, he consistently declined to outline future rate cuts or give explicit timelines for curbing persistent inflation. Trump remained lukewarm on the Fed's decision to hold interest rates steady but offered praise for the new chairman. — link

text: The End of Regulatory Arbitrage: Why It Matters for Crypto > Binance becoming regulated in more jurisdictions is not a threat to competitors. > Crypto competition has often been driven by regulatory arbitrage, where firms with lighter regulatory burdens gained an edge over those investing heavily in compliance and licensing. > As regulators bring Binance under increasingly consistent regulatory standards around the world, that advantage gradually disappears. > Competition should be based on products, technology, execution, governance, and trust, not on who can operate with the fewest rules. text: The money flow is the moat The best businesses sit inside the flow of value. Stablecoins and programmable networks are making that pattern available to every startup Historically, the most enduring businesses build defensive moats by positioning themselves directly within the flow of value to capture a percentage of transactions as network activity scales Blockchain technology and stablecoins make this model natively accessible to startups, enabling programmable, global financial rails that lower costs and capture revenue directly from circulating capital

text: Highlight Clip Coinbase CEO: Bitcoin Has Likely Bottomed Around $60K On June 11, 2026, Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong joined the Moonshots with Peter Diamandis podcast to share his outlook on Bitcoin's recent market performance. Armstrong analyzed that the short-term downturn was driven by three main factors: risk capital shifting toward the AI sector, the growing excitement around stablecoins due to regulatory clarity, and a cooling inflation outlook that made Bitcoin's traditional inflation trade less appealing. However, his instinct is that Bitcoin has likely bottomed out around the $60,000 mark. Armstrong believes these are purely short-term effects and that Bitcoin, as the new digital gold, will be a key part of the future economy. Remaining optimistic, he expects a much higher price by 2030 and states he is long on Bitcoin just like always. — link
